entries.intro: Something's been leaving tracks near {subject_focus_the}. Big ones, deep into the mud, and they don't match anything I've seen in thirty years of living here.
details: Three toes, clawed, wider than my hand spread flat. The stride between prints is longer than I am tall.
They circle the same area every night. Whatever it is, it's watching something.
The tracks stop at the tree line and don't come back out. Like it just vanished.
reactions: I don't scare easy, but I stopped walking that path after dark.
People keep saying it's a bear. It's not a bear. Bears don't walk in circles.

statCheck.pass: There were smaller tracks alongside the big ones, just for a stretch. Like something was being led.
statCheck.fail: The mud is too churned up out there. I already told you everything worth seeing.

entries.intro: A shepherd went missing near {subject_focus_the} about a week ago. His flock came back on their own, but he didn't.
details: His crook was found stuck in the ground, straight up, like someone planted it there on purpose.
The sheep were calm when they wandered back. Not spooked at all. That's the part that bothers me.
His dog showed up two days later. Won't stop whimpering and won't go near the eastern ridge.
reactions: His wife keeps a candle lit for him. I don't have the heart to tell her what I think happened.
We sent a search party, but nobody wanted to go past the ridge. Can't say I blame them.

entries.intro: Found three deer near {subject_focus_the}, all dead in a line. No wounds, no blood. Just lying there like they dropped mid-step.
details: Their eyes were open. All three facing the same direction, toward the old stone circle.
No scavengers touched them. Not a single crow, not a fox. Three days and nothing.
reactions: I've hunted these woods my whole life. Animals don't just die like that.
The butcher wouldn't take the meat. Said it smelled wrong, even though it looked fresh.

statCheck.pass: I didn't mention this to anyone else, but the grass under the deer was dead too. A perfect outline, like their shadow burned into the ground.
statCheck.fail: Whatever was there has been rained on and trampled since. I went back myself and there was nothing new to see.

entries.intro: There's a sound coming from the caves near {subject_focus_the} at night. Low and steady, like breathing, but too slow and too deep to be anything alive.
details: It starts just after sundown and fades before first light. Every single night for the past two weeks.
The ground near the cave mouth vibrates when it happens. You can feel it through your boots.
reactions: The children think it's a sleeping giant. I wish I could believe something that simple.
Two of the miners refuse to go back in until someone explains it. I can't say I disagree with them.

statCheck.pass: I listened carefully one night and realized the rhythm changes: it speeds up when someone gets close to the entrance, then slows again when they back off.
statCheck.fail: Whatever pattern exists is too faint to pick out from this distance. I have listened for hours and gotten nothing more.

entries.intro: The well water near {subject_focus_the} turned bitter overnight. Not just off-tasting: animals won't drink it, and the ones that did got sick.
details: Two goats and a mule drank from the trough before anyone noticed. The goats recovered, but the mule hasn't stood up since.
There's a faint sheen on the surface, oily-looking, and it doesn't mix when you stir it.
The deeper wells are fine for now. But the shallow ones all went bad at the same time.
reactions: We're hauling water from the river until someone figures it out. It's manageable, but it's a lot of extra work.
I keep thinking about what could poison three wells at once. Nothing good comes to mind.

entries.intro: There was a fire near {subject_focus_the} three nights ago. Burned a perfect circle into the hilltop, about twenty paces across. No lightning, no campfire, no explanation.
details: The ash is white, not gray. And the soil underneath is baked hard as pottery.
Everything inside the circle burned completely, but the grass right at the edge wasn't even singed.
reactions: Some people are calling it a sign. I'm calling it a reason to sleep with one eye open.
I've seen wildfires and I've seen lightning strikes. This wasn't either of those.

statCheck.pass: I went back to the circle at dusk and the ground was warm. Not from the sun: the warmth was coming from below, from deep down.
statCheck.fail: The ash has been scattered by wind and foot traffic. Hard to tell much from what is left now.

entries.intro: The wolves near {subject_focus_the} have been acting wrong. Not aggressive: the opposite. They sit at the edge of the village at night and just watch.
details: Six of them, every night, same spot by the tree line. They don't hunt, don't howl. Just sit there.
When you walk toward them, they don't run. They stand up, look at you, then sit back down.
The pack used to stay deep in the hills. Something drove them closer, and whatever it is, they seem more afraid of it than they are of us.
reactions: It's the stillness that gets to me. Wolves are supposed to move. These ones just stare.
Half the village thinks they're protecting us. The other half thinks they're waiting for something. Neither option helps me sleep.

entries.intro: A merchant heading through {subject_focus_the} turned back halfway and warned everyone not to take the eastern road. Wouldn't say why.
details: He was pale and his hands were shaking. This is a man who runs the northern passes in winter: he doesn't rattle easy.
He just said the road was wrong. That's the word he used: wrong. Not blocked, not dangerous. Wrong.
reactions: Two other traders have since gone around the long way without anyone asking them to. That tells me he wasn't exaggerating.
I pressed him for details over a drink, but he just shook his head and changed the subject.

statCheck.pass: After a few drinks he let one thing slip: he said the road was there, but the landmarks were in the wrong places. Like the land itself had shifted.
statCheck.fail: He will not say more. I pressed him once and he just gave me a long look and said he had already said more than he should have.

entries.intro: The birds around {subject_focus_the} left. All of them, all at once, three mornings ago. Haven't seen a single one since.
details: It wasn't migration: wrong season, wrong direction. They went south in a hurry, every species at once.
The nests are still full. Eggs, chicks, everything abandoned. They didn't plan to leave.
reactions: My grandmother used to say when the birds go quiet, you listen. When they leave, you follow.
The silence is the worst part. You don't realize how much noise they made until it's gone.

entries.intro: Someone found claw marks on the watchtower near {subject_focus_the}. High up, about fifteen feet off the ground, gouged deep into the stone.
details: Four parallel grooves, each one as wide as my finger. The stone around them is cracked like it was hit with a chisel.
They're only on the north face. Whatever made them came from one direction and didn't bother going around.
The guard on duty that night says he heard scraping but thought it was the wind. He's not sleeping well.
reactions: Stone doesn't scratch easy. Whatever did that had strength and intent. That combination worries me.
We doubled the night watch. Not because anyone thinks it'll help: because nobody wants to be alone up there.

statCheck.pass: I climbed up to look at the marks myself. There's a residue in the grooves: dark, almost like dried sap, but it smells like iron.
statCheck.fail: From the ground they just look like weathered stone. Someone would need to climb up there to see anything useful.
